guide.summary: This video follows a team chasing a hydraulic pressure problem that affects machine movement, especially the B-axis, and trying to bleed the hydraulic system. Along the way they compare book values to machine values, reset alarms, remove covers, and hunt for the vent ports and manifold shown in the diagrams.
guide.overview: What’s happening here is a hydraulic system is helping hold or move parts of the machine, and the team is focused on the B-axis and the pressure settings tied to it. You can see that the machine has a control side with alarms and reset functions, a hydraulic system with pressure set points, and an access area under covers where the manifold and vent ports should be. The basic rhythm of the job is: clear alarms, confirm the axis can move, compare the machine’s pressure settings to the documentation, and then bleed the hydraulic system if trapped air seems to be causing unstable behavior. You’ll also notice an important lesson from the video: the team does not blindly trust one drawing, because the book, the posted values, and the live machine readings do not all match. Before touching hydraulics, they think through what will move if pressure is lost, because one axis may rotate freely while another could drop.

machineIntro.title: B-axis
machineIntro.detail: The B-axis is the axis they are trying to move after clearing alarms. In this video, once the alarms are reset, movement of the B-axis is used as proof that the machine is making progress again. They also explain that if pressure is lost on the B-axis, it may rotate freely, which they say is not a big deal here because it is rotary.

machineIntro.title: Hydraulic system
machineIntro.detail: The hydraulic system is the oil-pressure system that helps this machine operate correctly. In the video, the team believes the hydraulics need attention because the set points do not look right and they suspect the system needs to be bled, meaning trapped air may need to be let out so pressure behaves normally again.

machineIntro.title: Set points and pressure ranges
machineIntro.detail: Set points are the target pressure values the machine uses to decide when a hydraulic condition is acceptable. The team says one pressure has to be between SP1 and RP1, and later they compare values from the book, the door, and the machine to decide what looks believable and what does not.

machineIntro.title: Manifold and vent ports
machineIntro.detail: The manifold is the hydraulic block where valves and passages are grouped together, and the vent ports are the openings the team expects to use or inspect while bleeding. They remove a larger cover because they believe the manifold is underneath and say they should see two ventilation ports for this hydraulic system.

machineIntro.title: Solenoid valve
machineIntro.detail: A solenoid valve is a valve moved by an electrical coil so the control system can direct hydraulic flow. In the diagram review, they identify a valve on the page as the solenoid valve while trying to understand which pressure values belong to which function.

troubleshooting.title: Book value does not match the machine setting
troubleshooting.symptom: You notice the machine shows 1,000 while the book says 1,300, and the pressure references in the documentation do not seem to agree with the machine.
troubleshooting.story:
Start by understanding the kind of mismatch they’re worried about. In the video, they compare live machine values to the book and later to a posted sheet on the door. That matters because hydraulic troubleshooting goes sideways fast if you chase the wrong target. A set point is only useful if it belongs to your exact machine condition.

What’s going wrong here is not a failed part you can point at right away. It’s uncertainty: one source says 1,300, another shows values like SP1 5 bar / 80 PSI and RP1 40 PSI, while the machine appears to be using values around 1,000 PSI. The team talks through the idea that factory testing may tune settings and that each machine may differ, but they also say the posted values on the door are probably the correct ones for this machine.

So the lesson is to compare all available references before changing anything. You’re looking for the source that matches the machine as built, not just the generic drawing. In this case, they specifically want to put one value back to 1,300 any time it gets changed, and they lean toward trusting the values posted on the door over the generic drawing.

fix.text: Compare the live machine value to the book and note the mismatch.
fix.expected: You confirm the machine shows 1,000 while the book shows 1,300.
fix.visual: the small illuminated pushbutton/indicator cluster on the upper-right black control block

fix.text: Put that value back to 1,300 when it has been changed.
fix.expected: The compared value matches the book target they call out.
fix.visual: the small black rotary control with yellow/red indicators near the upper-right center of the machine

fix.text: Review the pressure references for SP1, RP1, FH2, and FL2 on the diagram and compare them to what is on the machine.
fix.expected: You can see which values agree and which ones are completely different.
fix.visual: the pressure reference diagram on the printed schematic page

fix.text: Check the posted values on the door and use them as the likely correct reference for this machine.
fix.expected: You have a machine-specific reference to guide the next steps.
fix.visual: on the inside of the gray cabinet door with two white posted labels near the top center

troubleshooting.verify: You know this part is sorted when the value they called out is back at 1,300 and your chosen reference source is consistent enough to continue troubleshooting without guessing.
troubleshooting.ifNotResolved: If the book, drawing, and machine still do not agree, stop changing set points and get the fuller hydraulic connection diagram or the machine-specific information they were asking for further up the email chain.

troubleshooting.title: Right side light goes off and alarms need reset
troubleshooting.symptom: The right side light goes off and the machine will not behave normally until alarms are reset.
troubleshooting.story:
You’ll notice the team treats the light going off as a sign to go straight back to control. That tells you the machine has at least two alarm paths they care about here: a PLC alarm and a non-PLC alarm. PLC means the programmable controller that runs machine logic; in plain terms, it’s the machine’s traffic cop.

What’s happening here is they use the proper reset path, then a burst of alarms appears and disappears. They explain those were just testing, which is important because not every alarm burst means a fresh failure. After that, they use axis movement as the real proof that the reset worked.

So when you see a light go out like this, don’t start tearing into hydraulics first. Reset the control side correctly, watch what alarms remain, and then test whether the B-axis can move. That tells you whether you’re dealing with a control lockout, a temporary test alarm, or a deeper hydraulic issue.

fix.text: Go back to the control screen.
fix.expected: You are at the machine control where alarm reset can be performed.

fix.text: Reset the PLC alarm.
fix.expected: The PLC alarm reset is completed.
fix.visual: the KUKA KRC2 teach pendant screen at the top center of the pendant

fix.text: Reset the non-PLC alarm.
fix.expected: The non-PLC alarm reset is completed.
fix.visual: the row of small gray buttons below the monitor

fix.text: Watch for alarms that come up and then go away.
fix.expected: Temporary test alarms clear out.
fix.visual: the KUKA robot controller screen on the teach pendant unit

fix.text: Try moving the B-axis.
fix.expected: The machine moves, showing progress.
fix.visual: the robot control pendant screen on the right side of the cabinet

troubleshooting.verify: The fix is confirmed when the alarms clear and the B-axis moves again.
troubleshooting.ifNotResolved: If the light stays off or the B-axis still will not move, continue into the hydraulic checks and bleeding path shown later in the video.

troubleshooting.title: B-axis moves, but hydraulics still seem wrong
troubleshooting.symptom: The machine starts moving again, but the set points still do not look right and the team says hydraulics need attention.
troubleshooting.story:
This is a classic halfway-fixed situation. You get motion back, which feels like a win, but the machine still doesn’t look healthy. In the video, they say they do not like how the set points were changed and that they definitely need to do something with hydraulics.

Normally, once the axis moves, you’d expect the hydraulic support system to hold steady within the intended pressure range. Here, they’ve learned that one pressure needs to sit between SP1 and RP1, and they suspect trapped air is keeping the system from behaving normally. Air in a hydraulic system can make pressure feel soft or inconsistent because oil is meant to transmit force cleanly, while air compresses.

The way to spot this condition is exactly how they do it: movement returns, but confidence does not. The machine works enough to test, yet the pressure logic and set points still look suspicious. That’s when you stop celebrating and prepare to bleed the system.

fix.text: Confirm the machine can move again before doing hydraulic work.
fix.expected: You see movement and know the machine is responsive enough for the next step.
fix.visual: control panel screen on the KUKA robot cabinet

fix.text: Check the set points before changing anything else.
fix.expected: You know what the machine is currently using.
fix.visual: the KRC Expert 2 control screen in the center of the cabinet

fix.text: Confirm the pressure you are watching is supposed to be between SP1 and RP1.
fix.expected: You have the correct pressure range relationship in mind before bleeding.
fix.visual: the KUKA control pendant screen in the center of the console

fix.text: Plan to bleed the hydraulic system because trapped air is suspected.
fix.expected: You have a clear next diagnostic action.
fix.visual: the white hydraulic fitting on the right side of the machine

troubleshooting.verify: You’ve handled this stage correctly when you have motion restored, the suspect pressure range identified, and a controlled plan to bleed the system.
troubleshooting.ifNotResolved: If you still cannot identify the correct pressure relationship, pause and get the hydraulic diagram they request in the video before opening anything.

troubleshooting.title: Need to bleed the hydraulic system
troubleshooting.symptom: Hydraulic behavior seems wrong, the team suspects air in the system, and they begin opening covers to find the bleed points.
troubleshooting.story:
Here’s the heart of the repair. Bleeding means letting trapped air out of a hydraulic circuit so the oil can carry force properly again. In the video, they decide to bleed because the pressure behavior does not make sense and they have a feeling that’s the problem.

But notice how careful the conversation gets before anyone cracks a fitting. They point out that opening a hydraulic line will spray, especially if pressure is still in it. They also explain the consequence of losing pressure: on the B-axis it may rotate freely, which they accept, but on the Z-axis the whole axis could drop down. That’s the kind of thinking you want to copy every time—before you loosen anything, ask what gravity or stored pressure will do next.

Diagnosis here is partly physical and partly diagram-based. They shut hydraulics off, remove covers, look for the manifold, and expect to find two vent ports highlighted in the picture. They are not fully certain of the exact bleed control at the end of the clip, but they do establish the safe approach: isolate pressure, expose the manifold area, identify the vent ports from the diagram, and only then decide what to crack open slightly.

fix.text: Turn off coolant if it is not needed for this work.
fix.expected: Coolant is no longer running and the work area is easier to manage.
fix.visual: the coolant unit at the lower-left beside the control panel

fix.text: Turn off the hydraulics before opening the hydraulic circuit.
fix.expected: Hydraulic power is off before any line or fitting is loosened.
fix.visual: the round white access cover on the right-side machine panel

fix.text: Crack the hydraulic open only a little if you are bleeding it.
fix.expected: You create a small opening for bleeding rather than fully opening the circuit.
fix.visual: the hydraulic fitting on the upper-right side of the black housing

fix.text: Keep clear and be ready for spray when opening the hydraulic point.
fix.expected: You avoid getting hit by hydraulic spray.

fix.text: Think through which axis you are working on before bleeding.
fix.expected: You know whether free rotation or axis drop is a risk.
fix.visual: the small black bleed screw on the top of the gray housing

fix.text: Open the cover to access the hydraulic components.
fix.expected: The covered hydraulic area is exposed for inspection.
fix.visual: the black latch on top of the round gray cover

fix.text: Get and review the hydraulic diagram before going further.
fix.expected: You have a picture showing the manifold and the target ports.
fix.visual: the top-mounted black rectangular cover on the hydraulic unit

fix.text: Take off the big cover if needed to reach the manifold area.
fix.expected: You can see deeper into the hydraulic assembly.

fix.text: Look for the manifold under the cover.
fix.expected: You identify the hydraulic block area where the vent ports should be.
fix.visual: the round white manifold under the dark circular cover in the center

fix.text: Find the two vent ports shown in the picture.
fix.expected: You locate the highlighted vent ports the team says they are after.
fix.visual: the two small round vent ports on the upper-left side of the white housing

troubleshooting.verify: You know you are on the right track when the correct cover is off, the manifold is identified, and the two vent ports shown in the diagram are physically located.
troubleshooting.ifNotResolved: If you cannot identify the bleed point or vent ports, stop and get the fuller hydraulic overview or connection diagram they asked for, because the team says the exploded view alone is not enough.

troubleshooting.title: Cannot find the correct bleed point or diagram is unclear
troubleshooting.symptom: You have the cover off, but you still cannot clearly tell which part is the bleed point or whether the picture matches what you see.
troubleshooting.story:
This is a very real field problem: the machine is open, but the paperwork is not helping enough. In the video, they expected a non-exploded hydraulic overview, something more like a full schematic, but instead they keep getting partial pictures and even an electrical diagram. That slows everything down because you can’t confidently identify the manifold, vent ports, valve, or bleed point from a vague image.

What’s going wrong is not necessarily the machine now—it’s the information flow. They forward emails, screenshots, and pictures, rotate images, and compare what they see in the machine to what’s on the page. They eventually identify a solenoid valve on the diagram and the highlighted vent-port area, but by the end they are still unsure whether to pressurize, use a switch, or release a bolt.

The lesson for you is simple: if you cannot clearly identify the target part, do not guess with hydraulics. Use the diagram to narrow it down, remove the correct cover, compare the physical layout to the picture, and keep escalating for a clearer machine-specific diagram when needed.

fix.text: Forward the hydraulic information and pictures to the person helping you.
fix.expected: Everyone is looking at the same reference material.

fix.text: Take off the big cover so the visible machine layout matches the diagram better.
fix.expected: More of the hydraulic assembly is exposed.

fix.text: Use the picture to identify the manifold cover and the highlighted vent-port area.
fix.expected: You narrow the search to the correct section of the hydraulic system.
fix.visual: the large round gray manifold cover in the center of the machine with the vent-port holes on its front-left side

fix.text: Ask for the full hydraulic overview or connection diagram if the exploded view is not enough.
fix.expected: You get a more useful reference for tracing the system.

fix.text: Compare the physical machine to the diagram until you can identify the valve shown as the solenoid valve.
fix.expected: At least one known component on the diagram matches what you see on the machine.

fix.text: Stop before loosening bolts or fittings if you still cannot clearly identify the bleed point.
fix.expected: You avoid opening the wrong part of the hydraulic system.
fix.visual: the cluster of hydraulic fittings and blue hoses on top of the metal tank

troubleshooting.verify: This is resolved when the picture and the machine agree well enough that you can point to the manifold, vent ports, and valve with confidence.
troubleshooting.ifNotResolved: If the references still do not match, keep working from machine-specific photos and the posted machine information rather than guessing from a generic drawing.

safety.title: Hydraulic spray when opening the system
safety.detail: They warn that if you crack the hydraulic open, it is going to spray. Pressurized hydraulic fluid can come out forcefully, so you need to control the opening and keep yourself out of the line of fire.
steps: Turn the hydraulics off before opening the circuit.
Crack the fitting open only a little.
Expect spray and keep your body clear.

safety.title: B-axis may rotate freely if pressure is lost
safety.detail: They explain that if pressure is lost on the B-axis, it may rotate freely. In this case they say that is probably fine, but you still need to expect movement when hydraulic holding pressure is removed.
steps: Think through what the axis will do before bleeding.
Keep clear of the rotating path.
Support or control the setup so weight does not shift unexpectedly.

safety.title: Z-axis can drop if pressure is lost
safety.detail: They give a stronger warning for the Z-axis: if you work on it and lose pressure, the whole axis is going to drop down. That is a serious gravity hazard.
steps: Be really careful when working on the Z-axis.
Do not release pressure on the Z-axis unless you have accounted for the drop risk.
Stop if you are not sure which axis the hydraulic circuit is supporting.

glossary.definition: An alarm tied to the programmable controller, the machine control that manages logic and sequences.
A rotary machine axis that turns a part or assembly.
A machine axis that moves up and down.
A pressure system that uses oil to move or hold machine parts.
To let trapped air out of a hydraulic system.
A target value the machine uses for control, such as a pressure threshold.
A hydraulic block that routes fluid through internal passages to valves and ports.
An opening used to vent or bleed air from part of the hydraulic system.
A valve operated by an electrical coil so the control system can switch hydraulic flow.
A diagram showing how electrical parts are connected.

chunks.videoSummary: This video follows a team chasing a hydraulic pressure problem that affects machine movement, especially the B-axis, and trying to bleed the hydraulic system. Along the way they compare book values to machine values, reset alarms, remove covers, and hunt for the vent ports and manifold shown in the diagrams.

domainMetaData.summary: This video follows a team chasing a hydraulic pressure problem that affects machine movement, especially the B-axis, and trying to bleed the hydraulic system. domainMetaData.overview: What’s happening here is a hydraulic system is helping hold or move parts of the machine, and the team is focused on the B-axis and the pressure settings tied to it. You can see that the machine has a control side with alarms and reset functions, a hydraulic system with pressure set points, and an access area under covers where the manifold and vent ports should be. The basic rhythm of the job is: clear alarms, confirm the axis can move, compare the machine’s pressure settings to the documentation, and then bleed the hydraulic system if trapped air seems to be causing unstable behavior. You’ll also notice an important lesson from the video: the team does not blindly trust one drawing, because the book, the posted values, and the live machine readings do not all match. Before touching hydraulics, they think through what will move if pressure is lost, because one axis may rotate freely while another could drop.

machineIntro.title: B-axis
machineIntro.detail: The B-axis is the axis they are trying to move after clearing alarms. In this video, once the alarms are reset, movement of the B-axis is used as proof that the machine is making progress again. They also explain that if pressure is lost on the B-axis, it may rotate freely, which they say is not a big deal here because it is rotary.

machineIntro.title: Hydraulic system
machineIntro.detail: The hydraulic system is the oil-pressure system that helps this machine operate correctly.
